source.title: Biosensor and machine learning-aided engineering of an amaryllidaceae enzyme

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: The paper aims to engineer the amaryllidaceae enzyme norbelladine 4'-O-methyltransferase (Nb4OMT) for improved production of 4'-O-methylnorbelladine (4NB) in E. coli, using a biosensor-machine learning technology stack. The initial problem is the slow process of biocatalyst engineering for therapeutic alkaloids, and the need for high-throughput screening and efficient enzyme design. The biosensor is developed via directed evolution of RamR to specifically detect 4NB, and the enzyme is engineered using MutComputeX, a structure-based residual neural network. The process involves iterative cycles of biosensor evolution, enzyme activity monitoring, ML-guided design, experimental validation, and structural analysis.

section_gists.heading: Biosensor and machine learning-aided engineering of an amaryllidaceae enzyme
section_gists.gist: Introduction and overview: The paper proposes a biosensor-ML stack to engineer an Amaryllidaceae enzyme in E. coli. A biosensor for 4NB is evolved from RamR, and MutComputeX is developed to design Nb4OMT variants. Improved variants show 60% higher titer, 2-fold higher catalytic activity, and 3-fold lower off-product. A crystal structure explains key mutations.
process_signals: Initial problem: slow biocatalyst engineering for therapeutic alkaloids
Hypothesis: biosensor and ML can accelerate enzyme engineering
Goal: improve microbial fermentation of 4NB
methods: Directed evolution of RamR into 4NB biosensor
Development of MutComputeX (structure-based residual neural network)
Screening of ML-designed Nb4OMT variants with biosensor

section_gists.heading: Identifying a biosensor for the branchpoint amaryllidaceae alkaloid 4'-O-methylnorbelladine
section_gists.gist: RamR is evolved into a specific biosensor for 4NB. Initial RamR shows slight response to 4NB and norbelladine. Docking suggests key residues. Three site-saturated libraries are generated and screened via SELIS. After round 1, variant 4NB1.2 (K63T, L66M) shows 20-fold selectivity for 4NB. Further evolution with counter-selection against norbelladine yields 4NB2.1 with high specificity and sensitivity (EC50=20 μM).
process_signals: Starting point: RamR, a malleable TetR-family repressor
Initial observation: RamR slightly responsive to 4NB and norbelladine (3.8-fold and 4.4-fold)
Hypothesis: docking pose informs rational library design
Library design: three NNS libraries targeting inward-facing residues
Screening method: SELIS (growth-based selection then fluorescence screen)
Round 1: variant 4NB1.2 (K63T, L66M) with 20-fold selectivity, but other variants had higher sensitivity
Decision: use 4NB1.2 as starting point for further evolution due to selectivity
Round 2: additional libraries with counter-selection against norbelladine
Outcome: 4NB2.1 with high specificity and sensitivity (EC50=20 μM)
methods: SELIS (Seamless Enrichment of Ligand Inducible Sensors)
failures_or_limits: Some variants had higher sensitivity but lower selectivity, not chosen

section_gists.heading: Monitoring norbelladine methyltransferase activity in Escherichia coli
section_gists.gist: The evolved biosensor (pSens4NB2) is used to monitor Nb4OMT activity in E. coli. Fluorescence correlates with enzyme expression, substrate concentration, and 4NB titer. However, HPLC shows significant leftover norbelladine and LC/MS identifies 3'-O-methylnorbelladine off-product, indicating low catalytic efficiency and regiospecificity. Directed evolution via error-prone PCR fails to identify improved variants.
process_signals: Observation: biosensor can monitor Nb4OMT activity in vivo
Finding: wild-type Nb4OMT has low catalytic efficiency and poor regiospecificity
Hypothesis: enzyme engineering can improve activity and specificity
Attempt: directed evolution via error-prone PCR (avg 3 mutations/gene)
Failure: no variants outperformed wild-type in liquid-based screen
Decision: pursue ML-guided design as complementary approach
methods: Co-transformation of pSens4NB2 and Nb4OMT expression plasmid
Plate-based fluorescence screen followed by liquid-based secondary screen
metrics: Significant leftover norbelladine even at 50 μM supplementation
failures_or_limits: Directed evolution (error-prone PCR) failed to find improved variants

section_gists.heading: Developing a machine learning pipeline for structure-based enzyme engineering
section_gists.gist: MutComputeX is developed as an improved structure-based neural network. It is trained on ~2.3M microenvironments from >23,000 structures, including protein-ligand interfaces. It uses a 3DResNet architecture with new atomic channels (phosphorus, halogens). The model achieves ~80% wild-type accuracy on a 250K test set. An ensemble of three models (MutComputeX) is selected based on correlation with FireProtDB ΔTM data. To generate designs, an AlphaFold2 model of Nb4OMT is docked with SAH and norbelladine using GNINA. MutComputeX predicts substitutions; designs are selected based on predicted amino acid probabilities.
process_signals: Problem: previous MutCompute could not handle non-protein atoms
Solution: rebuild data pipelines, add atomic channels, use residual CNN
Training: ~2.3M microenvironments, including ~256K protein-ligand interfaces
Model selection: ensemble of three models based on zero-shot correlation with FireProtDB
Input structure: AlphaFold2 model of Nb4OMT docked with SAH and norbelladine
Docking constraints: D155 or K158 as general base, cation-π interaction with K158
Design generation: MutComputeX predicts amino acid likelihoods; substitutions with high probability for non-wild-type amino acids are selected
metrics: Wild-type accuracy: ~80% on 250K test set (3DResNet), 67.3% overall for MutComputeX ensemble
Previous 3DCNN: 69% on 6K test set, 62.1% on interface
failures_or_limits: MutComputeX has lower wild-type accuracy than individual 3DResNets, but better correlation with experimental data

section_gists.heading: Characterization of improved norbelladine methyltransferase variants
section_gists.gist: 22 MutComputeX-designed variants are screened using the biosensor at three temperatures and two substrate concentrations. A53M consistently outperforms wild-type; H17K, H17R, S159E, V203E, E36P-G40E show improvement in some conditions. Double/triple mutants with A53M give highest signals. Time course shows faster fluorescence increase for A53M variants. LC/MS confirms 60% increase in 4NB, 17-fold decrease in leftover norbelladine, and 3-fold reduction in 3'-O-methylnorbelladine off-product for E36P-G40E-A53M. A53M is only predicted when docking is included; without docking, A53R is predicted and reduces activity. Kinetic analysis: A53M increases kcat/Km ~2-fold (kcat >2.1-fold increase), Tm +1.7°C; triple mutant Tm +5.6°C.
process_signals: Screening: 22 mutants across 3 temperatures and 2 substrate concentrations
Key finding: A53M consistently beneficial
Observation: A53M only predicted with docked ligands; without docking, A53R predicted (detrimental)
Validation: fluorescence correlates with LC/MS product titer
Kinetic and thermal characterization confirms improvements
failures_or_limits: A53R predicted without docking reduced activity
Some single mutants (H17K, etc.) only improved in certain conditions

section_gists.heading: Crystal structure of an improved norbelladine methyltransferase
section_gists.gist: Crystal structure of Nb4OMT E36P/G40E/A53M with SAH solved at 2.4 Å. The enzyme is a homodimer. Overall fold matches AlphaFold2 except N-terminus: AlphaFold2 predicted Lys13 salt bridge with Asp155, Asp181, Asn182, but crystal structure shows these coordinate a Ca2+ ion and Lys13 interacts with Tyr186 and Tyr194. A53M inserts into hydrophobic pocket (Trp50, Tyr81, Tyr108), stabilizing active site. E36P-G40E shifts glutamate from 36 to 40, preserving salt bridge with Lys118 and capping helix. Docking norbelladine into crystal structure shows Ca2+ positions substrate, and A53M may influence regiospecificity.
process_signals: Goal: understand mechanism of beneficial mutations
Finding: discrepancy between AlphaFold2 and crystal structure in N-terminus
Structural basis for thermostability: A53M hydrophobic packing, E36P-G40E salt bridge and helix capping
Insight: Ca2+ in active site not predicted by AlphaFold2
failures_or_limits: AlphaFold2 model inaccurate in N-terminal region and missed Ca2+

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: Summary: evolved biosensor enables precise monitoring of 4NB; MutComputeX is first structure-based model for sequence substitutions trained on non-protein atoms. Limitations: only 22 variants tested; manual curation of designs; reliance on AlphaFold2 and docking. Future: apply to other enzymes and pathways; further improve biosensor and ML pipeline.
process_signals: Biosensor specificity emerged after only four substitutions
MutComputeX generalizes to protein-ligand interfaces
Acknowledged limitations: small number of variants, manual design selection, dependence on predicted structure
failures_or_limits: Reliance on AlphaFold2 and docking accuracy

section_gists.heading: Methods
section_gists.gist: Methods section heading, no content provided.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The research follows a linear but iterative process: first develop a specific biosensor, then use it to monitor enzyme activity, then apply ML to design improved enzyme variants, and finally validate and structurally characterize the best variants.
The failure of random mutagenesis (error-prone PCR) motivated the development and application of MutComputeX.
The inclusion of ligand atoms in MutComputeX training was critical for predicting beneficial active site mutations (A53M vs A53R).
The crystal structure revealed inaccuracies in the AlphaFold2 model, highlighting the importance of experimental validation.
uncertainties_to_resolve: What were the exact selection conditions and stringency in each round of biosensor evolution?
How many variants were screened in the error-prone PCR library, and what was the screening cutoff?
What were the specific criteria for selecting the 22 MutComputeX designs from the predictions?
Were any other ML models or design strategies attempted before MutComputeX?
What is the full kinetic characterization of all variants, and were any compensatory mutations observed?
How does the biosensor performance compare to other detection methods (e.g., LC/MS) in terms of throughput and accuracy?

events.summary: Slow biocatalyst engineering is a major challenge for industry-scale biomanufacturing of therapeutic alkaloids.
Proposing a biosensor-machine learning technology stack can accelerate biocatalyst development for Amaryllidaceae enzymes.
Development of MutComputeX, a structure-based residual neural network, to generate activity-enriched variants of a plant methyltransferase.
Screening of MutComputeX-guided Nb4OMT designs with the evolved biosensor, identifying variants with improved product titer, catalytic activity, and reduced off-product.
Crystal structure of engineered enzyme elucidates mechanism behind key beneficial mutations and highlights discrepancies with AlphaFold2 model.
Wild-type RamR shows slight responsiveness to 4NB and norbelladine, with 3.8-fold and 4.4-fold fluorescence increases.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers introduction and early results, establishing the problem, hypothesis, and initial design steps for biosensor and ML pipeline.

events.summary: The 4NB2.1 sensor was highly selective for 4NB over norbelladine, displaying an over 80-fold preference.
Docking suggests K63T repositions hydroxyl to hydrogen bond with Y59, L66M strengthens hydrophobic pocket around 4'-O-methyl group.
The 4NB2.1 sensor is approximately 10-fold more sensitive than the HPLC method, making it well-suited for screening.
Wild-type Nb4OMT has low catalytic efficiency and poor regiospecificity, with significant leftover norbelladine and off-product 3'-O-methylnorbelladine.
Directed evolution via error-prone PCR failed to identify variants outperforming wild-type Nb4OMT in liquid-based screen.
numbers_or_metrics: average of three mutations per gene

events.summary: MutComputeX was developed to handle non-protein atoms, using a 3DResNet architecture and new atomic channels, achieving ~80% wild-type accuracy.
22 MutComputeX-designed variants screened across three temperatures and two substrate concentrations; A53M consistently outperformed wild-type.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk covers biosensor validation, monitoring of Nb4OMT activity, failure of directed evolution, development of MutComputeX, and initial screening of ML-designed variants.

events.summary: LC/MS analysis confirms that the E36P-G40E-A53M variant increases 4NB product by 60% and decreases remnant norbelladine 17-fold.
The A53M mutation reduces the 3'-O-methylnorbelladine off-product by about 3-fold.
The beneficial A53M substitution was only predicted by MutComputeX when the structure model was docked with SAH and norbelladine; without docking, A53R was predicted and reduced activity.
Steady-state kinetics show A53M increases kcat/Km by about 2-fold due to >2.1-fold increase in kcat, and Tm by 1.7°C.
The triple mutant E36P/G40E/A53M shows a 5.6°C increase in Tm relative to wild-type.
Crystal structure of Nb4OMT E36P/G40E/A53M with SAH solved at 2.4 Å reveals a homodimer and discrepancies with AlphaFold2 in the N-terminal region.
Structural analysis shows A53M inserts into a hydrophobic pocket, stabilizing the active site, and E36P-G40E preserves a salt bridge while capping an alpha helix.
Docking norbelladine into the crystal structure suggests Ca2+ positions the substrate and a sulfur-π interaction with Met53 may improve regiospecificity.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains key experimental validation and structural characterization of ML-designed variants, directly supporting the paper's core narrative of enzyme engineering.

events.summary: Crystal structure of engineered Nb4OMT solved at 2.4 Å reveals dimeric assembly and active site details.
Comparison of N-termini shows discrepancy between crystal structure and AlphaFold2 model.
A53M mutation inserts into hydrophobic pocket, stabilizing active site.
Active site with SAH and docked norbelladine shows Ca2+ positioning substrate and A53M influencing regiospecificity.
Active site of solved structure differs from AlphaFold2 models due to lack of metal ions, ligands, and cofactors.
AlphaFill tool did not incorporate norbelladine substrate or provide more accurate model.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains crystal structure analysis and discussion of MutComputeX validation and limitations.

events.summary: MutComputeX model architecture and training details are described, including dataset generation and benchmarking.
Mutations were designed with two goals: stabilizing the protein away from the active site and investigating point mutations where predictions differed between docked and apo structures.
LC-MS method for analyzing products and residual substrate from whole-cell biotransformation.
Kinetic data were fit using KinTek Explorer with a minimal model; substrate inhibition was observed for A53M and triple mutant.
X-ray crystallography of Nb4OMT triple mutant (E36P+G40E+A53M) with SAH solved at 2.4 Å.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains detailed methods for model training, mutational design, HPLC, LC-MS, kinetics, protein purification, and crystallography, which are essential for understanding the experimental and computational processes.
This chunk contains only references, acknowledgements, author contributions, competing interests, and additional information. No research evolution events are described.

evidence_bank.quote: A major challenge to achieving industry-scale biomanufacturing of therapeutic alkaloids is the slow process of biocatalyst engineering.
Here, we propose an efficient biosensor-machine learning technology stack for biocatalyst development, which we apply to engineer an Amaryllidaceae enzyme in Escherichia coli.

A structure-based residual neural network (MutComputeX) is subsequently developed and used to generate activity-enriched variants of a plant methyltransferase, which are rapidly screened with the biosensor.
Functional enzyme variants are identified that yield a 60% improvement in product titer, 2-fold higher catalytic activity, and 3-fold lower off-product regioisomer formation.
A solved crystal structure elucidates the mechanism behind key beneficial mutations.
RamR was found to be slightly responsive to both 4NB and its immediate precursor norbelladine, yielding 3.8-fold and 4.4-fold increases in fluorescence, respectively
the 4NB2.1 sensor was highly selective for 4NB over norbelladine, displaying an over 80-fold preference for the former

the K63T substitution repositions the hydroxyl group at position 3 of 4NB to hydrogen bond with the wild-type Y59 residue, while the L66M substitution strengthens a hydrophobic pocket around the 4'-O-Methyl group of 4NB
the 4NB2.1 sensor is approximately 10-fold more sensitive than the HPLC method, making it well-suited for screening transplanted biosynthetic enzymes from plants
HPLC analysis indicated that a significant amount of supplemented norbelladine remained after culturing for 24 h
LC/MS analysis identified 3'-O-Methylnorbelladine as a minor component, indicating that the wild-type Nb4OMT enzyme was not highly regiospecific
this approach was not able to identify variants that outperformed the wild-type enzyme in the liquid-based screen
The ensembled 3DResNet model (MutComputeX) had an overall wild-type accuracy of 67.3%
In all tested conditions, the A53M mutation consistently produced a fluorescent signal significantly above the wild-type enzyme
the level of 4NB product increased by 60% while the level of remnant norbelladine decreased 17-fold
the A53M mutation reduced levels of the 3'-O-Methyl-norbelladine off-product by about 3-fold

the beneficial A53M substitution was only predicted by MutComputeX when the Nb4OMT structure model was docked with SAH and norbelladine; in contrast, A53R was predicted when docking was not performed, a substitution that reduced activity
The A53M substitution increased $k_{cat}/K_{m}$ by a factor of about 2, due to a >2.1-fold increase in $k_{cat}$ , and increased the $T_{m}$ by 1.7 °C
a 5.6 °C increase in $T_{m}$ relative to the wild-type Nb4OMT enzyme
we determined the structure of the Nb4OMT $^{E36P/G40E/A53M}$ variant in complex with S-adenosyl-L-homocysteine (SAH) at 2.4 Å resolution
The overall fold of the protein was almost identical to the predicted AlphaFold2 structure, except for the N-terminal region
The A53M substitution inserts a larger hydrophobic methionine inside the hydrophobic pocket formed by Trp50, Tyr81, and Tyr108
The E36P-G40E double mutant shifts a glutamate from position 36 to position 40 and thereby preserves the salt bridge interaction with Lys118 while proline capping the alpha helix

A sulfur- $\pi$ interaction between the catechol group of norbelladine and Methionine 53 may also restrict the rotation of the catechol group, thereby reducing the cross-methylation of the 3' position and improving specificity
Global structure of Nb4OMT $^{E36P/G40E/A53M}$ solved in 2.4 A resolution.
Comparison of N-termini of Nb4OMT crystal structure (orange) and wild-type Nb4OMT AlphaFold2 structure (blue).
Local context of the A53M mutant residue.
Active site context of Nb4OMT $^{E36P/G40E/A53M}$ in complex with S-Adenosyl-L-homocysteine (SAH) and docked with norbelladine.
Interestingly, the active site of the solved structure differs from AlphaFold2 models, likely due to lack of the metal ions, ligands, and cofactors.
While the recently described AlphaFill tool could potentially address this issue for some models, it did not incorporate the norbelladine substrate or provide a more accurate model of Nb4OMT
A total of 2,569,256 microenvironments were sampled from 22,584 protein sequences and split 90:10 to generate our training and test set splits
Mutations were designed with two goals: stabilizing the protein away from the active site and investigating point mutations where predictions differed between the docked and apo protein structures.

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Slow biocatalyst engineering for therapeutic alkaloids; need for high-throughput screening and efficient enzyme design.

canonical_events.summary: Slow biocatalyst engineering is a major challenge for industry-scale biomanufacturing of therapeutic alkaloids.
Proposing a biosensor-machine learning technology stack can accelerate biocatalyst development for Amaryllidaceae enzymes.
Wild-type RamR shows slight responsiveness to 4NB and norbelladine (3.8-fold and 4.4-fold fluorescence increases).
Directed evolution of RamR into a specific 4NB biosensor via site-saturated mutagenesis and SELIS screening.
Round 1 yields variant 4NB1.2 (K63T, L66M) with 20-fold selectivity for 4NB over norbelladine.
Further evolution with counter-selection yields 4NB2.1 with high specificity and sensitivity (EC50=20 μM, >80-fold preference for 4NB).
Wild-type Nb4OMT has low catalytic efficiency and poor regiospecificity, with significant leftover norbelladine and off-product 3'-O-methylnorbelladine.
Directed evolution via error-prone PCR fails to identify variants outperforming wild-type Nb4OMT.
key_numbers: average of three mutations per gene

canonical_events.summary: Development of MutComputeX, a structure-based residual neural network trained on ~2.3M microenvironments including protein-ligand interfaces, achieving ~80% wild-type accuracy.
MutComputeX designs 22 Nb4OMT variants using AlphaFold2 structure docked with SAH and norbelladine; designs include A53M and E36P-G40E.
Screening of 22 MutComputeX-designed variants with biosensor across three temperatures and two substrate concentrations; A53M consistently outperforms wild-type.
LC/MS confirms E36P-G40E-A53M variant increases 4NB product by 60%, decreases remnant norbelladine 17-fold, and reduces 3'-O-methylnorbelladine off-product by 3-fold.
A53M substitution only predicted by MutComputeX with docked ligands; without docking, A53R predicted and reduces activity.
Steady-state kinetics show A53M increases kcat/Km ~2-fold (kcat >2.1-fold increase) and Tm by 1.7°C; triple mutant Tm increases by 5.6°C.
Crystal structure of Nb4OMT E36P/G40E/A53M with SAH solved at 2.4 Å reveals homodimer and discrepancies with AlphaFold2 in N-terminal region.
Structural analysis shows A53M inserts into hydrophobic pocket stabilizing active site; E36P-G40E preserves salt bridge and caps helix; Ca2+ positions substrate.
Only 22 variants tested; manual curation of ML predictions; reliance on AlphaFold2 and docking accuracy; AlphaFold2 model inaccurate in N-terminal region and missed Ca2+.

edges.reason: The problem motivates the hypothesis of using a biosensor-ML stack.
The hypothesis leads to testing RamR as a starting point for biosensor development.
Initial responsiveness of RamR justifies directed evolution to improve specificity.
Directed evolution round 1 produces 4NB1.2 with improved selectivity.
4NB1.2 is further evolved to yield 4NB2.1 with higher specificity and sensitivity.
The evolved biosensor enables monitoring of Nb4OMT activity, revealing its poor performance.
Low catalytic efficiency motivates directed evolution attempt.
Failure of random mutagenesis motivates development of MutComputeX for ML-guided design.
MutComputeX is used to design Nb4OMT variants.
Designed variants are screened using the biosensor.
Promising variants are validated by LC/MS for product titer and specificity.
The beneficial A53M mutation is analyzed to understand the importance of docking in ML predictions.
Kinetic and thermal characterization quantifies the improvements.
Crystal structure is solved to elucidate the mechanism of beneficial mutations.
Structural analysis explains the molecular basis for improved activity and stability.
Structural insights highlight limitations of the computational models used.

must_cover_points: Failure of error-prone PCR for Nb4OMT
Development of MutComputeX with ligand-aware training
ML-guided design and screening of Nb4OMT variants
Identification of A53M as key beneficial mutation
Importance of docking for predicting A53M
Quantitative improvements in titer, kinetics, and thermostability
Crystal structure validation and mechanistic insights
Limitations of AlphaFold2 and manual design curation
